name: Eyosiyas Taye

bio: I am a problem solver at heart who explores the field of Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning and general automation using programming. What I lack in experience I make up in the willingness to learn and great energy :D

certifications.name: Data Analysis and Reporting in SAS Visual Analytics

projects.name: PokR
projects.description: A real-time poker odds assistant that uses YOLO-based computer vision to detect playing cards from a webcam or screen capture, then automatically populates a poker odds calculator. Trained on a custom dataset across multiple YOLO model variants.

projects.name: Accountability Module
projects.fullTitle: Accountability Module: Increasing Trust in Reinforcement Learning Agents
projects.subtitle: MSc Thesis — University of Oslo, 2023
projects.description: MSc thesis: an external explainability module for reinforcement learning agents. Built to answer why an autonomous vehicle crashed — using saliency maps, evolutionary algorithms, and UMAP counterfactuals to make black-box AI decisions transparent and auditable.

detail.abstract:
Artificial Intelligence requires trust to be fully utilised by users and for them to feel safe while using them. Trust, and indirectly, a sense of safety, has been overlooked in the pursuit of more accurate or better-performing black box models. The field of Explainable Artificial Intelligence and the current recommendations and regulations around Artificial Intelligence require more transparency and accountability from governmental and private institutes.

Creating a self-explainable AI that can be used to solve a problem while explaining its reasoning is challenging to develop. Still, it would be unable to explain all the other AIs without self-explainable abilities. It would likely not function for different problem domains and tasks without extensive knowledge about the model.

The solution proposed in this thesis is the Accountability Module. It is meant to function as an external explanatory module, which would be able to function with different AI models in different problem domains. The prototype was inspired by accident investigations regarding autonomous vehicles but was created and implemented for a simplified simulation of vehicles driving on a highway. The prototype's goal was to attempt to assist an investigator in understanding why the vehicle crashed.

The Accountability Module found the main factors in the decision that resulted in an accident. It was also able to facilitate the answering of whether the outcome was avoidable and if there were inconsistencies with the agent's logic by examining different cases against each other. The prototype managed to provide useful explanations and assist investigators in understanding and troubleshooting agents.

The thesis and the Accountability Module indicate that a similar explanatory module is a robust direction to explore further. The chosen explainability methods and techniques were highly connected to the problem domain and limited by the scope of the thesis. Therefore, a more extensive test of the prototype with different problems needs to be performed to check the system's rigidity and versatility as well as the significance of the results. Nevertheless, in a collaboration between an Accountability Module expert and a domain expert, I expect a modular explainability solution to create more insight into an AI model and its significant incidents.

images.caption: Figure 4.1 — A frame from the Highway environment. The green box is the agent's car, while the yellow boxes are the other cars. All the cars are tasked with driving to the right.

Figure 4.2 — Image a) is of the Greyscale observation, where four cars are present. Image b) is the Kinematic observation. The y-axis values are E for the Ego vehicle and 1–4 for the four closest vehicles. The x-axis are p (presence), X and Y (positions relative to the Ego vehicle), and Xv and Yv (velocities).
Figure 5.1 — A brief overview of the Accountability Module, containing its inputs, methods and the results. The AM is built up by the three sub-modules used to answer α, β and γ.

Figure 5.2 — Image a) is of the Kinematic observation. Image b) is the Saliency observation. The bluer spots indicate a value closer to one (high focus). The redder spots indicate a value closer to zero (low focus). The X positions of vehicles one and two are the most important factors in the decision.
Table 5.1 — A quick summary of the research objectives, questions, and methods used.
Figure 6.2 — A sharp focus example showing an agent focusing on the X position of vehicle one. Blue means a value closer to one (high focus), red means a value closer to zero (low focus).

Figure 7.5 — Saliency Observation from five different 1,000,000 iteration agents at the same episode and frame. Three of them focus on the presence, while the remaining two only focus on the X position of the closest vehicle.

Figure 7.6 — UMAP Kinematic clustering of agent DQN-0 at 1,000,000 iterations. The circles show the span of the furthest point from the cluster's centre. Blue circle = furthest point, red = furthest within 1.5 IQR, green = furthest within two standard deviations.

Figure 7.7 — Greyscale (a) and Kinematic (b) observations for counterfactual pairs of actions four and two. Top row shows the furthest UMAP points; bottom row shows the closest. Both allow for a better understanding of the observations' similarities and differences.
Figure 7.8 — UMAP Saliency clustering of agent DQN-0 at 1,000,000 iterations. The axes and circles use the same logic as the Kinematic UMAP, while distances and similarities are different.
Figure 7.9 — Greyscale (a) and Saliency (b) observations for the same counterfactual pairs as Figure 7.7.

projects.name: SpotR
projects.description: A mobile workout tracker built for the gym. Log exercises, track sets and reps, visualise progress over time with charts. Built with React Native and local SQLite storage — no account needed. Currently in development, heading to the app stores.

projects.name: GrocR
projects.description: A Norwegian grocery price comparison app. A Python scraper pulls live product data (prices, nutrition, images) from grocery stores, feeding a React Native app with barcode scanning and category browsing. Project on pause — planned revival.

experience.description: Data engineering on modern cloud-based data platforms.

Large-scale migration from legacy on-prem SAS to a modern cloud architecture (SAS Viya, Snowflake, Azure). Built automated monitoring tools and dashboards covering 500+ critical tables using Azure Data Factory, Azure Monitor Logs, and Snowflake. Developed ETL pipelines, API integrations, Delta Lake processing in Databricks, and custom Python scripts for data transformation. Handled incident management for business-critical reporting systems.

Built ETL pipelines and transferred data between databases. Performed data analysis and visualization against specified KPIs, and identified new relevant KPIs. Developed a machine learning prototype using time-series forecasting. Stack: PostgreSQL, Python, Miro.
Teaching assistant across three courses: high-level programming (Python, Bash, Regex), mechatronics, and introduction to computer technology.
